production_mt.source: FAOSTAT global potato production 2022 (374.8 Mt); China 95.5 Mt (25.5%), India 56 Mt (15.0%)

density_g_per_cm3.source: raw potato specific gravity ~1.05-1.09 (potatoes sink in water)

tier1_fates.name: Fresh / table food
tier1_fates.source: residual of FBS food line after country-weighted processing; CIP: fresh ~2/3 of harvest, concentrated in China/India/RoW

tier1_fates.name: Processing (food)
tier1_fates.source: Run-2 country-weighted rebuild: China 15% x India 7% x EU ~45% x US 70% x RoW ~8%, production-weighted = ~17%

tier1_fates.name: Animal feed
tier1_fates.source: FAO FBS utilisation accounts; large share used as feed (culls/surplus)

tier1_fates.name: Seed (replanting)
tier1_fates.source: FAO FBS; potatoes have a high seed rate (tubers held back for planting)

tier1_fates.name: Waste & loss (accounted)
tier1_fates.source: FAO FBS 'losses' line (post-harvest accounted; distinct from full-chain FLW overlay)

tier1_fates.name: Industrial (non-food)
tier1_fates.source: native/modified starch, alcohol (vodka/aquavit), bioplastics; larger in developed producers

country_processing.note: Production-weighted processing share (Run-2 rebuild). Weighted mean = 17.2%.

rows.source: China ~15% processed; >2/3 fresh; net FF exporter 2022
NL/BE/DE/FR 60-80% processed; E-EU more fresh; blended ~45%
USDA: processed ~70% of US availability since 2010

tier2_processing.name: Frozen (fries/wedges/tots)
tier2_processing.source: USDA: US frozen = 176M cwt raw (61% of US processing); frozen is the largest global processed form

tier2_processing.name: Crisps / chips
tier2_processing.source: USDA: US chips/shoestring 57.8M cwt (~20% of US processing); larger share in some snack-heavy markets

fries_within_frozen_pct.source: fries dominate frozen potato products; remainder wedges/tots/hash browns

fries_finished.note: Fries measured as FINISHED product (~50% weight yield from raw potato: peel/trim/moisture). Distinct from the 29.8 Mt RAW-potato fries stream used for MUSHT.

yield_from_raw_pct.source: typical finished-fry weight yield from raw potato

fry_processors.note: The big three corroborate the ~30 Mt raw / ~15 Mt finished global fries scale. Company tonnages mix finished-product and marketing claims — treat as scale corroboration, not exact.

rows.claim: makes ~1 in 4 fries worldwide; ~$9B sales; world's largest frozen-potato maker
world #2 frozen-potato maker; largest in North America (~$6B+ sales)
~3 Mt of potatoes/yr; key McDonald's fry supplier

restaurant_fry_volume.note: Split of FOODSERVICE fry volume between chains and the independent/non-chain tail. Independents are ~422k of ~685k US units (60-70%) and ~67% of restaurant sales; chains (top 500) ~33% of sales but over-index on fries, so their fry-volume share (~45%) exceeds their sales share.
restaurant_fry_volume.retail_vs_foodservice_note: Separately, a large share of fries is RETAIL (frozen bags cooked at home), outside this foodservice split.

fry_cut_mix.note: Share of the fries stream by cut geometry (Run-2 deep dive, US-foodservice-weighted). Straight cut ~48% of the total product-type market and higher within QSR fries. MUSHT miles are straight-line-equivalent (curly/waffle are not literal 0.75cm straight lines).

cuts.name: Straight / shoestring
cuts.exemplar_chains: McDonald's, Burger King, Wendy's, Five Guys, In-N-Out

cuts.name: Curly
cuts.exemplar_chains: Arby's, Jack in the Box, Hardee's

tps.note: Taters Per Second — potatoes produced per second (production basis). Cross-universe nod to Universe of Finance's TPS (transactions/second).

avg_potato_weight_g.source: global-average tuber across all sizes/varieties; a US medium russet is ~170-180 g but developing-country potatoes run smaller

monthly_index.note: Northern-Hemisphere-weighted consumption seasonality (multipliers; tool normalises the day-weighted mean to 1.0 so the 12 months reconcile to the annual TPS). Peaks at the winter holidays, dips post-holiday, summer QSR-fry bump.
monthly_index.global_note: Both-hemisphere global index = 1 + 0.4 x (NH index - 1). Damped because Southern-Hemisphere seasons offset the North by 6 months and most of the world's potato eaters (India, China, tropics) have weak seasonality. The holiday spike is largely a Northern/Western phenomenon — global TPS is far flatter.

drivers.Nov: Thanksgiving — 75% of people eat potatoes; ~214M lb moved pre-holiday

restaurant_tiers.note: Fry-cut character by restaurant tier. The independent/non-chain tail is the largest single fry-volume tier and the most varied — the visible chains are the short head. Shares 🔴.

tiers.name: QSR / limited-service
tiers.typical_cut: straight/shoestring dominant; signature exceptions (waffle, curly, tots)
tiers.exemplars: McDonald's (straight), Burger King (straight), Wendy's (natural-cut), Chick-fil-A (waffle), Arby's (curly), Sonic (tots/crinkle)

tiers.name: Fast casual
tiers.typical_cut: 'better fry' — fresh hand-cut or premium crinkle
tiers.exemplars: Five Guys (fresh hand-cut), In-N-Out (fresh straight), Shake Shack (crinkle), Culver's (crinkle), Steak 'n Shake (shoestring), Whataburger (straight), Wingstop (thin straight)

tiers.name: Casual dining / full-service
tiers.typical_cut: steak fries, seasoned, crinkle; sides-menu variety
tiers.exemplars: Red Robin (steak/crinkle, bottomless), Applebee's, Chili's, Texas Roadhouse (steak fries), Outback

tiers.name: Fine dining / gastropub
tiers.typical_cut: hand-cut pommes frites, triple-cooked chips, truffle fries
tiers.exemplars: Belgian-style frites houses, steak-frites bistros, gastropubs

tiers.name: Independent / non-chain (the long tail)
tiers.typical_cut: everything — diner hand-cut & crinkle, chip-shop chips, street-vendor, pub wedges; the widest cut variety of any tier
tiers.exemplars: diners, UK/IE chippies, food trucks, street vendors, indie burger joints (~half of restaurant units, majority of global fry volume)

fry_companions.note: Tracker — food items most frequently sold WITH a fry (share of fry-accompanied orders). v1 estimates 🔴. Beverage (soda) is near-universal; ketchup is the #1 dip.

ubiquitous_pairings.regional_dips: mayo (BE/NL), malt vinegar (UK/IE), gravy+cheese=poutine (CA), aioli, ranch

ppc.note: PPC — Pennies Per Calorie for a fry (US cents / calorie = price_cents / calories). Portion-independent (a ratio), so medium chain fries and whole retail bags compare directly. Where is a fry calorie cheapest to buy? Prices US 2025 (🟡); calories from published nutrition (🟢).

fry_index.note: The Fry Index — international PPC using a McDonald's medium fries (320 cal) as a common unit, à la the Big Mac Index. Local price converted to USD (2025 FX). Where is a fry calorie cheapest on Earth?

fry_flation.note: Fry-flation — US McDonald's medium fries nominal price over time (320 cal). cpi_index is US CPI-U (1982-84=100); the tool deflates to base_year 2025 dollars for the REAL series.

consumed_potato_index.note: CPI — the Consumed Potato Index: potato's share of a country's dietary energy SUPPLY (as-eaten, incl. frying oil for fries), with a fries-only sub-share. Supply-based (FAO FBS style, includes some waste) — an upper bound on intake. Reveals the staple-vs-fry-culture divide: highest potato-dependence is NOT highest fry-dependence.

countries.note: the fry nation — most potato calories are fried

income_deciles.note: Nuanced: fast-food FREQUENCY rises with income, but lower-income diets draw a slightly higher SHARE of away-from-home calories from cheap fried potato (ERS: 16.4% vs 15.3%). Net: potato-calorie share falls gently with income; fry share is flatter, tilting slightly to the bottom decile via cheap retail frozen. Modeled 🔴.

value_add.note: The Value-Add Battery — how many ways to measure the value of turning a potato into a fry. Anchored on MCE-PF (income-elasticity added by processing) and VAM (price multiple over the farm-gate potato).

elasticity.note: Income elasticity of demand. Potato is a textbook inferior good; fries/processed are ~normal. MCE-PF = e_fry - e_potato = elasticity ADDED by processing.

potato.source: potato income elasticity -0.4 to -1.19 (inferior good); demand migrates fresh->processed as income rises

fry.source: processed/fries ~normal good; resists the income decline that hits fresh potato

ppc_ref.note: pennies/calorie from the PPC deep dive; DIY vs served = the willingness-to-pay premium

fry_bill.note: The Fry Bill — the induced supply chain. (1) how much potato demand exists ONLY because of the fry (counterfactual via MCE-PF), and (2) the sector-by-sector input bill to keep the world eating potatoes as fries.

induced_demand.note: Without the fry format, how much of the fries stream would simply not be eaten? Model: a share reverts to fresh boiled potato; the rest exits to non-potato foods (the income-elastic demand the fry sustains — see MCE-PF).

inputs.context: ~0.9% of all vegetable oil humanity produces (~220 Mt)
~180,000 tonnes of edible salt on the world's fries
~1.4x Ireland's annual electricity; electricity is 70-93% of processing GHG
~8.5 km3/yr — mostly green (rain) crop water; 574 L per kg frozen fries
~900,000 full-time-equivalent jobs frying and serving the world
~2 Mt of cartons, bags and boxes
~67 Mt CO2e — a small nation's footprint; frozen fries are the highest-emission potato product

fry_economy.note: The Fry Economy — the total economic impact of the effort to fry potatoes and keep people eating them as incomes rise. Direct + indirect (suppliers) + induced (worker spending), IMPLAN/Type-II style.

final_demand_usd_b.source: ~135B servings x ~$1.2 blended consumer price; frozen-fry manufacturing market alone ~$26B (65% foodservice) sits inside this

value_added_share.note: value-added (GDP) as a share of total output

direct_jobs.source: frying + serving + processing labor (~1.8B hrs / 2000, from the Fry Bill)

employment_multiplier.source: food manufacturing ~3 total jobs/direct; blended down for the service-heavy mix

induced_jobs.note: jobs from direct+indirect workers spending their wages

food_loss_waste_overlay.note: Cross-cutting overlay along field->consumer chain. NOT summed into tier1_fates (would double-count with the FBS 'losses' line). Reported as the Conscience Number.

chain_loss_pct.source: Roots & tubers have the highest FLW of any commodity group (>25%); regional range 33% (N.Africa/W&C Asia) to 60% (N.America/Oceania). Potato harvest often 'reduced by half from field to fork'. Fresh ~30% disposed vs processing ~4-5%.
